cards.title: Lambda Expression Basics
cards.explanation: A lambda expression is a concise way to represent an anonymous function that can be passed around as a value. It consists of a parameter list, an arrow (->), and a body. Lambdas can only be used where a functional interface (an interface with exactly one abstract method) is expected.
cards.whyItMatters: Lambdas are the foundation of Java's functional programming features and are used everywhere in Streams, CompletableFuture, and event handling.
cards.gotcha: Lambdas can only capture effectively final local variables. If you try to modify a captured variable inside a lambda, the compiler will throw an error.

cards.title: Built-in Functional Interfaces
cards.explanation: Java provides four core functional interfaces in java.util.function: Function<T,R> (takes T, returns R), Consumer<T> (takes T, returns void), Supplier<T> (takes nothing, returns T), and Predicate<T> (takes T, returns boolean). These cover most use cases and are the building blocks for the Stream API.
cards.whyItMatters: Understanding these four interfaces is enough to read and write 90% of lambda-heavy Java code.
cards.gotcha: Function.andThen() vs Function.compose() — andThen applies the second function after, compose applies it before. Getting them backwards is a common bug.

cards.title: Stream Pipeline: Source → Intermediate → Terminal
cards.explanation: A Stream pipeline has three parts: a source (collection, array, or generator), zero or more intermediate operations (filter, map, sorted — these are lazy and return a new Stream), and exactly one terminal operation (collect, forEach, reduce — this triggers evaluation). Streams are single-use: once a terminal operation is called, the stream is consumed and cannot be reused.
cards.codeExample:
List<String> names = List.of("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Dave");

List<String> result = names.stream()          // source
    .filter(n -> n.length() > 3)              // intermediate (lazy)
    .map(String::toUpperCase)                 // intermediate (lazy)
    .sorted()                                 // intermediate (lazy)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());            // terminal (triggers eval)

// result: [ALICE, CHARLIE, DAVE]
cards.whyItMatters: Streams replace verbose for-loops with readable, composable pipelines and enable parallel processing with a single .parallel() call.
cards.gotcha: Intermediate operations are lazy — no work happens until a terminal operation is called. Calling .filter() alone does nothing and won't throw even on a null stream source.

cards.title: Collectors: groupingBy, partitioningBy, toMap
cards.explanation: The Collectors utility class provides pre-built collectors for common aggregation patterns. groupingBy partitions elements into a Map by a classifier function. partitioningBy is a special case that splits into true/false groups. toMap converts a stream into a Map with key and value extractor functions.
cards.whyItMatters: groupingBy is used constantly for reporting, analytics, and data transformation. It replaces nested loops and temporary maps.
cards.gotcha: toMap throws IllegalStateException on duplicate keys. Use the merge function overload: Collectors.toMap(keyFn, valueFn, (v1, v2) -> v1) to handle duplicates.

cards.title: Optional — Avoiding NullPointerException
cards.explanation: Optional<T> is a container that may or may not hold a value. It forces callers to explicitly handle the absent case, making null-related bugs visible at compile time. Use it as a return type when a method legitimately might return nothing. Do not use it as a field type, method parameter, or in collections.
cards.whyItMatters: Properly used, Optional makes APIs self-documenting about nullability and eliminates entire classes of NPE bugs.
cards.gotcha: Optional.get() without isPresent() check throws NoSuchElementException — just as bad as NPE. Always use orElse, orElseGet, or ifPresent instead.
